request.question: When was the man who saved 20,000 Jews hanged?

title: Traian Popovici
Hideki Tojo
Ettefagh Synagogue
Będzin Ghetto
Giovanni Palatucci
Raoul Wallenberg Holocaust Memorial Park
Rescue of Jews by Catholics during the Holocaust
History of the Jews in Belarus
Marvin Tokayer
The Bielski Brothers (book)
sentences: Traian Popovici (October 17, 1892 – June 4, 1946) was a Romanian lawyer and mayor of Cernăuţi during World War II, known for saving 20,000 Jews of Bukovina from deportation.

Hideki Tōjō (Kyūjitai: 東條 英機 ; Shinjitai: 東条 英機 ; "   "; December 30, 1884 – December 23, 1948) was a general of the Imperial Japanese Army (IJA), the leader of the Imperial Rule Assistance Association, and the 27th Prime Minister of Japan during much of World War II, from October 17, 1941, to July 22, 1944.

As Prime Minister, he was responsible for ordering the attack on Pearl Harbor, which initiated war between Japan and the United States, although planning for it had begun in April 1941 before he entered office.
After the end of the war, Tojo was arrested, sentenced to death for Japanese war crimes by the International Military Tribunal for the Far East, and hanged on December 23, 1948.
After the Second World War, with the installation of a new government in Iraq, the situation of the Jews in Iraq deteriorated.
After prominent members of the Jewish community such as Laura Khozoee were arrested, and Shafiq Adas, a successful Jewish businessman, was hanged, many Iraqi Jews decided to emigrate.
Some left for Israel while others decided to settle in Iran.
They reached Iran with the help of Kurdish Jews and many arrived at Khorramshahr.
With the help of Harun Abdolnabi, a prominent Jew in Khorramshahr, some were able to go to Tehran and obtain legal documents.
From 1946 to 1951 many Iraqi Jews moved to Tehran, while others moved to Europe and the United States.
At this time, with the help of Iranian Jews, the Iraqi Jews decided to create a cultural center.
In 1946 Meir Abdullah Batson bought a large piece of land with an area of over 5000 m2 near the University of Tehran, in Ghods Street.
He separated a piece of land of over 865 m2 and turned it into a synagogue, with the help of Saleh and Davood Mashi, Heskel Haim, and others.
The community wanted to name the synagogue after Meir Abdullah, but the government did not accept it because he was a foreign citizen.
Hence the name "Ettefagh" was chosen, and it became the main place of gathering for the Iraqi Jews of Tehran.
The synagogue architecture was designed in the style of Babylonian architecture, and it was fitted with the most advanced cooling system of the time.
Most of the Torah scrolls in the synagogue were brought by the Jews who came from Iraq, and a Babylonian Talmud in Aramaic script was dedicated to the synagogue.
Many Iraqi Jews emigrated from Iran during the Iranian revolution, and today only a few families (mainly Iranian Jews) still use the synagogue.

The Będzin Ghetto (a.k.a. the Bendzin Ghetto, Yiddish: בענדינער געטאָ‎ , Bendiner geto; German: "Ghetto von Bendsburg" ) was a World War II ghetto set up by Nazi Germany for the Polish Jews in the town of Będzin in occupied south-western Poland.
The formation of the 'Jewish Quarter' was pronounced by the German authorities in July 1940.
Over 20,000 local Jews from Będzin, along with additional 10,000 Jews expelled from neighbouring communities, were forced to subsist there until the end of the Ghetto history during the Holocaust.

Most of the able-bodied poor were forced to work in German military factories before being transported aboard Holocaust trains to the nearby concentration camp at Auschwitz where they were exterminated.

The last major deportation of the ghetto inmates by the German SS – men, women and children – between 1 and 3 August 1943 was marked by the ghetto uprising by members of the Jewish Combat Organization.

Giovanni Palatucci (31 May 1909 – 10 February 1945) was an Italian police official who was long believed to have saved thousands of Jews in Fiume between 1939 and 1944 (current Rijeka in Croatia) from being deported to Nazi extermination camps.

In 2013 a research panel of historians led by the "Centro Primo Levi" reviewed almost 700 documents and concluded that Palatucci had followed RSI and German orders concerning the Jews and enabling the deportation of the majority of the 570 Jews living in Fiume and surrounding areas, 412 of whom were deported to Auschwitz, a higher percentage than in any Italian city.
The matter is currently the topic of scholarly debate.

A national commission of historians recommended by the Union of the Italian Jewish Communities, the Center for Contemporary Jewish Documentation in Milan, the Italian Ministry of the Interior and the Centro Primo Levi NY is conducting a comprehensive review of the documents.

The Raoul Wallenberg Emlékpark (memory park) in the rear courtyard of the Dohány Street Synagogue holds the "Memorial of the Hungarian Jewish Martyrs" — at least 400,000 Hungarian Jews were murdered by the Nazis.
Made by Imre Varga, it resembles a weeping willow whose leaves bear inscriptions with the names of victims.

There is also a memorial to Wallenberg and other Righteous Among the Nations, among them: Swiss Vice-consul Carl Lutz; Giorgio Perlasca, an Italian man who, with a strategic escamotage, declared himself the Spanish consul, releasing documents of protection and current passports to Jews in Budapest without distinction (he saved five thousand); Mons.

Angelo Rotta, an Italian Prelate Bishop and Apostolic Nuncio of the State of Vatican City in Budapest, which issued protective sheets, misrepresentations of baptism (to save them from forced labor) and Vatican passports to Jews, without distinction of any kind present in Budapest (saving fifteen thousand), who saved, with his secretary Mons.
Gennaro Verolino tens of thousands of Hungarian Jews during World War II.

The Roman Catholic Church - through lobbying of Axis officials, provision of false documents, and hiding of people in monasteries, convents, schools, among families and the institutions of the Vatican itself - saved hundreds of thousands of Jews from being murdered by the Nazis during the Holocaust.
The Israeli diplomat and historian Pinchas Lapide estimated the figure at between 700,000 and 860,000 - although the figure is contested.
The Catholic Church itself faced persecution in Hitler's Germany, and institutional German Catholic resistance to Nazism centred largely on defending the Church's own rights and institutions.
Broader resistance tended to be fragmented and led by individual effort in Germany - but in every country under German occupation, priests played a major part in rescuing Jews.

Aiding Jews met with severe penalty and many rescuers and would-be rescuers were killed - as with St Maximillian Kolbe, Giuseppe Girotti and Bernard Lichtenberg who were sent to the Concentration Camps.
The Jews in Belarus were the third largest ethnic group in the country in the first half of the 20th century.
Before World War II, Jews were the third among the ethnic groups in Belarus and comprised more than 40% of the population in cities and towns.
The population of cities such as Minsk, Pinsk, Mahiliou, Babrujsk, Viciebsk, and Homiel was more than 50% Jewish.
In 1939 there were 375,000 Jews in Belarus, or 13.6% of the total population.
Some 246.000 Jews of 375.000—66% of the Jewish population—were killed in Belarus during the Holocaust.
According to the 2009 census, there were 12,926 Jews in Belarus (0.1% of the population).
The Jewish Agency estimates the community of Jews in Belarus at 20,000.
Marvin Tokayer (born 1936) is an American Rabbi and author.
Tokayer was a United States Air Force chaplain in Japan.
He was then advised to go to Japan as the only rabbi by the Lubavitcher Rebbe.
He then became rabbi to the Jewish community of Japan for eight years.
He has written 20 books in Japanese.
He has researched the history of the Jews in China and Japan and the role that the Japanese played in helping victims of the Holocaust.
In the 2010s, he has collaborated with Kenneth X. Robbins in editing studies of expatriate communities in Asia.
He praises General Hideki Tojo and General Kiichiro Higuchi who saved 20,000 Jews who were sending back to Germany by the USSR, and are disappointed at the fact that this history is not known.
<ref>　<ref>
The Bielski Brothers: The True Story of Three Men Who Defied the Nazis, Built a Village in the Forest, and Saved 1,200 Jews is a non-fiction book by Peter Duffy, which was published in 2003.

It tells the story of Tuvia Bielski, Alexander Zeisal Bielski(Zus), Aharon Bielski, and Asael Bielski, four Jewish brothers who established a large partisan camp in the forests of Belarus during World War II, and so saved 1,200 Jews from the Nazis.
The book describes how, in 1941, three brothers witnessed their parents and two other siblings being led away to their eventual murders.
The brothers fought back against Germans and collaborators, waging guerrilla warfare in the forests of Belarus.

By using their intimate knowledge of the dense forests surrounding the towns of Lida and Novogrudek, the Bielskis evaded the Nazis and established a hidden base camp, then set about convincing other Jews to join their ranks.
The Germans came upon them once but were unable to get rid of them.
As more Jews arrived each day, a robust community began to emerge; a "Jerusalem in the woods".
In July 1944, after some 30 months in the woods, the Bielskis learned that the Germans, overrun by the Red Army, were retreating back toward Berlin.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Agent role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

Pipeline position: after summary1 and before second-hop retrieval. Inputs are the original question and the first-hop intermediate state. Functional responsibility: produce one focused retrieval query that is likely to recover the missing evidence needed by the downstream agents. Query quality is determined by evidence recovery, not by whether the query itself resembles or contains the final answer.

Native output contract: The module must consume `question` and `summary_1` and produce exactly one compact second-hop query field named `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include every distinct entity token visible in the inputs in the single terse query and place at most one neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the targeted relation or attribute (for example: "EntityA EntityB director").
- If a visible disambiguator or attribute token appears adjacent to an entity in the inputs (for example a multiword service name, an explicit page title, a year, or an attribute marker like a birth/producer/service/series cue), include that disambiguator token together with the associated entity in the query rather than dropping or truncating it.
- If `summary_1` contains an explicit page-title token sequence (for example an instruction-like ExactPageTitle or Fetch page title), prefer that exact page-title token sequence as the query value.
- When the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise plain-text summary string (no extra fields, labels, markup, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification.
- Primary goal: explicitly integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the supported linkage it needs.

Explicit bridging requirement:
- When the passages supply a clear linking fact between the context entity and information in the passages, produce a concise 1–2 sentence integrated summary that: (1) names the context entity using the exact form present in `context`, (2) names the supporting entity or attribute using the exact form present in `passages`, and (3) states the explicit relation (membership, creator attribution, parentage, partnership, award, representation, founding, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Phrase this as one short bridging sentence when possible.
- For relations that naturally involve two distinct facts (for example, parentage plus representation, or a person linked to a show and also to an occupation), it is acceptable to use two short sentences within the same single-string summary: e.g., one sentence for parentage and one for representation. Still observe the 1–2 sentence limit.

Inclusion of passage-supplied disambiguators (only when directly relevant):
- If a passage supplies canonical multi-token names or other disambiguating attributes needed to resolve the question, include them inline using the wording from the passages: append birthdates in parentheses immediately after the associated full canonical name (e.g., Name (YYYY–) ), state group type and formation year using the passage's phrasing (e.g., "a [group type] formed in [year]"), and include a canonical member list exactly as shown (comma-separated) only when the passage presents a definitive membership list. Do not add ancillary attributes that are not necessary to the identification or explicit linking required by the question.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (e.g., "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages, comma-separated if multiple. Do not add explanatory clauses, relations, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (for example: "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Preserve uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 short sentences). Do not expand into biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Use only facts and phrasing present in `context` and `passages`; do not introduce or infer unsupported information.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.
- Do not assert that a passage's member list is complete unless the passage explicitly states it is definitive.

Format enforcement:
- Return a single plain-text string only. Do not emit JSON, dictionaries, labels, bullets, or any structured artifact—only the concise natural-language summary string.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else. Before emitting that string, you may apply only the narrowly permitted normalizations listed below. After applying permitted normalizations, the emitted string must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim (see normalization constraints). Specific allowed single-token answers: for a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no. For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces), subject only to the permitted normalizations. Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if a permitted normalization applies (see below).

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) and the permitted normalizations as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Permitted normalizations and extraction rules (apply only when the token(s) to emit appear verbatim in the prioritized summary and only to produce a strictly smaller, but still verbatim-supported, minimal label):
- Parenthetical/core-name stripping: if the candidate label in the prioritized summary includes common corporate suffixes or parenthetical abbreviations (for example variants like "Inc.", "LLC", or parenthetical acronyms), strip those suffixes/parentheticals and emit the core name only (do not invent or substitute other words).
- Hyphenated composite phrase extraction: if the prioritized summary presents a composite genre or label in the form "<modifier> X-Y" (for example an extra leading modifier followed by a hyphenated core like "classic action-adventure"), emit only the hyphenated core token exactly as shown ("X-Y").
- Trailing generic-descriptor removal: if the candidate minimal answer appears as a proper-name followed by a single trailing generic descriptor token of the form "<ProperName> crisis" (or an equivalent single-word generic classifier), strip the trailing generic token and emit only the proper-name sequence.
- Denomination-occupation reduction: if the question's scope requests a denomination/kind and the prioritized summary contains the form "<denomination> priest" (or equivalent denomination + occupational noun), emit only the denomination token(s) (i.e., the minimal denomination label).

Normalization constraints:
- Do not apply normalizations unless the text to emit appears verbatim in the prioritized summary (summary_2, or summary_1 only if summary_2 is silent for the answer).
- Normalizations may only remove or trim tokens; they must not add, invent, or substitute new content.
- Do not globally strip generic classifier nouns from multi-token geographic names or other proper nouns unless covered by the permitted rules above.

Tie-break and variant-selection rules:
- If a role-descriptor and a proper name co-occur in the prioritized summary in the form "<role> <Name>", prefer emitting the named individual exactly as shown (emit the Name only) unless the prioritized summary presents the role as the requested concise label explicitly without a name.
- For title or label-variant conflicts, prefer an alternate or parenthetical variant only when the prioritized summary explicitly marks that variant as an alias, alternate name, or the original-language title (or otherwise explicitly labels it as a variant) AND that variant matches the question's scope. If no such explicit labeling appears, emit the primary label supported by summary_2.

Strict emission rule (verbatim-emission):
- The final `answer` must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim after applying only the permitted normalizations above. Do not append or prepend occupational nouns, role descriptors, location qualifiers, or explanatory tokens that are not part of that verbatim label.

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

By using their intimate knowledge of the dense forests surrounding the towns of Lida and Novogrudek, the Bielskis evaded the Nazis and established a hidden base camp, then set about convincing other Jews to join their ranks.
The Germans came upon them once but were unable to get rid of them.
As more Jews arrived each day, a robust community began to emerge; a "Jerusalem in the woods".
In July 1944, after some 30 months in the woods, the Bielskis learned that the Germans, overrun by the Red Army, were retreating back toward Berlin.

response.hop1_query: When was the man who saved 20,000 Jews hanged?
hop1_docs: Raoul Wallenberg Holocaust Memorial Park | The Raoul Wallenberg Emlékpark (memory park) in the rear courtyard of the Dohány Street Synagogue holds the "Memorial of the Hungarian Jewish Martyrs" — at least 400,000 Hungarian Jews were murdered by the Nazis.  Made by Imre Varga, it resembles a weeping willow whose leaves bear inscriptions with the names of victims.  There is also a memorial to Wallenberg and other Righteous Among the Nations, among them: Swiss Vice-consul Carl Lutz; Giorgio Perlasca, an Italian man who, with a strategic escamotage, declared himself the Spanish consul, releasing documents of protection and current passports to Jews in Budapest without distinction (he saved five thousand); Mons.  Angelo Rotta, an Italian Prelate Bishop and Apostolic Nuncio of the State of Vatican City in Budapest, which issued protective sheets, misrepresentations of baptism (to save them from forced labor) and Vatican passports to Jews, without distinction of any kind present in Budapest (saving fifteen thousand), who saved, with his secretary Mons.  Gennaro Verolino tens of thousands of Hungarian Jews during World War II.

The Hanging Tree | The Hanging Tree is a 1959 Technicolor Western film directed by Delmer Daves.  Karl Malden took over directing duties for several days when Daves fell ill.  The film stars Gary Cooper, Maria Schell, George C. Scott and Malden and is set in the gold fields of Montana during the gold rush of the 1860s and '70s.  The story follows a doctor who saves a criminal from a lynch mob, then learns of the man's past and tries to manipulate him.
Traian Popovici | Traian Popovici (October 17, 1892 – June 4, 1946) was a Romanian lawyer and mayor of Cernăuţi during World War II, known for saving 20,000 Jews of Bukovina from deportation.

Necdet Kent | İsmail Necdet Kent (1 January 1911 – 20 September 2002) was a Turkish diplomat who risked his life to save Jews during World War II.  While vice-consul in Marseilles, France between 1941 and 1944, he gave documents of citizenship to dozens of Turkish Jews living in France who did not have proper identity papers, to save them from deportation to the Nazi gas chambers.

Behiç Erkin | Behiç Erkin (1876 in Constantinople, Ottoman Empire-November 11, 1961 in Istanbul, Turkey) was a career Army officer; first director (1920–1926) of the Turkish State Railways, nationalized under his auspices; and statesman with the Turkish government who helped save almost 20,000 ethnic Jews in France during World War II.  He was Minister of Public Works, 1926–1928, and deputy for three terms; and an ambassador.  He served as Turkey's ambassador to Budapest between 1928–1939, and to Paris and Vichy between August 1939-August 1943.  As Turkish ambassador in France under the German Occupation after June 1940, Erkin used the power of his office and nation's neutrality to save Jews who could document a Turkish connection, however slight, from the Holocaust.

Drobytsky Yar | Drobytsky Yar is a ravine in Kharkiv, Ukraine.  In December 1941, Nazi troops invading the Soviet Union began killing local residents over the following year.  At the end of this period, some 16,000 people, mainly Jews were killed.  Notably on December 15, 1941, when the temperature was −15 degrees Celsius (+5 °F), ca. 15,000 Jews were shot.  Children were thrown into pits alive, to save bullets, in expectation that they would quickly freeze to death.

Marvin Tokayer | Marvin Tokayer (born 1936) is an American Rabbi and author.  Tokayer was a United States Air Force chaplain in Japan.  He was then advised to go to Japan as the only rabbi by the Lubavitcher Rebbe.  He then became rabbi to the Jewish community of Japan for eight years.  He has written 20 books in Japanese.  He has researched the history of the Jews in China and Japan and the role that the Japanese played in helping victims of the Holocaust.  In the 2010s, he has collaborated with Kenneth X. Robbins in editing studies of expatriate communities in Asia.  He praises General Hideki Tojo and General Kiichiro Higuchi who saved 20,000 Jews who were sending back to Germany by the USSR, and are disappointed at the fact that this history is not known.  <ref>　<ref>

Ambiguities:

- Variant wording: "20,000" vs "almost 20,000"; unclear which individual the question refers to.

Bridge / missing fact:

Retrieval direction:

- Open page "Traian Popovici" to confirm whether he was hanged or his cause/date of death.
- Open page "Behiç Erkin" to confirm death details and whether he was ever hanged.

hop2_docs: Yuko Tojo | Yuko Tojo (東條 由布子 , "Tōjō Yūko" , May 20, 1939 (Keijo, Japanese Korea) – February 13, 2013, Tokyo, Japan) was a Japanese ultra-nationalist politician, Imperial Japanese apologist, and brief political hopeful.  She was the granddaughter of General Hideki Tōjō, the Japanese wartime prime minister who was convicted of planning and orchestrating most of the major Japanese acts of aggression of World War II; for these crimes, Hideki Tojo was convicted as a Class A war criminal and hanged after World War II.

Hideki Tojo | Hideki Tōjō (Kyūjitai: 東條 英機 ; Shinjitai: 東条 英機 ; "   "; December 30, 1884 – December 23, 1948) was a general of the Imperial Japanese Army (IJA), the leader of the Imperial Rule Assistance Association, and the 27th Prime Minister of Japan during much of World War II, from October 17, 1941, to July 22, 1944.  As Prime Minister, he was responsible for ordering the attack on Pearl Harbor, which initiated war between Japan and the United States, although planning for it had begun in April 1941 before he entered office.  After the end of the war, Tojo was arrested, sentenced to death for Japanese war crimes by the International Military Tribunal for the Far East, and hanged on December 23, 1948.

Tojo (song) | "Tojo" a.k.a. "Tojo Never Made it to Darwin" was the second single by iconic Australian rock group Hoodoo Gurus and was released on Big Time Records and distributed by EMI in June 1983.  It was written by their lead singer-guitarist, Dave Faulkner.  It was also featured on their first album "Stoneage Romeos" (1984), which was produced by Alan Thorne.  Tojo in the title refers to the World War II Japanese General and Prime Minister Hideki Tōjō.

Senjinkun military code | The Instructions for the Battlefield (戦陣訓 , Senjinkun ) was a pocket-sized military code issued to soldiers in the Imperial Japanese forces on 8 January 1941 in the name of then-War Minister Hideki Tojo.  It was in use at the outbreak of the Pacific War.

Pride (1998 film) | Pride (プライド 運命の瞬間; , Puraido: Unmei no Toki ) , also known as Pride: The Fateful Moment, is a 1998 Japanese historical drama directed by Shunya Itō.  The film, based on the International Military Tribunal for the Far East of 1946–48, depicts Japanese prime minister Hideki Tojo (played by Masahiko Tsugawa) as a family man who fought to defend Japan and Asia from western colonialism but was ultimately hanged by a vengeful United States.  Shot at a cost of ¥1.5 billion and partially funded by a right-wing businessman, "Pride" was one of the highest-grossing Japanese films of 1998 and was nominated for two Japan Academy Prizes.  Although the filmmakers intended the film to open dialogue on Japanese history, it was controversial in China, South Korea, and Japan owing to concerns of revisionism.

Shōhei Hino | Shōhei Hino (Japanese: 火野 正平 , Hepburn: Shōhei Hino , born May 30, 1949 in Tokyo) is a Japanese actor.  Hino was born in Tokyo and raised in Osaka.  Hino is well known for his roles in the Hissatsu series.  He played General Hideki Tojo in the film "Emperor".

Saburō Kurusu | Saburō Kurusu (来栖 三郎 , Kurusu Saburō , March 6, 1886- April 7, 1954) was a Japanese career diplomat.  He is remembered now as an envoy who tried to negotiate peace and understanding with the United States while the Japanese government under Hideki Tojo was secretly preparing the attack on Pearl Harbor.
